d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Sparta and Persia sign the Peace of Antalcidas (preliminary)
v.s: Spartan King Agesilaus II had campaigned for years in Asia Minor against the Persian satrap Tissaphernes, failing to capture the coastal Greek city-states. Threatened by Thebes, Corinth and other poleis at home, Agesilaus was forced to conclude a temporary truce with Persia. Under the agreement, Sparta recognized Persian suzerainty over the Greek cities of coastal Asia Minor, and Persia stopped funding Athens and Thebes against Sparta. This treaty ended Sparta's banner of 'liberating the Greeks of Asia Minor' and marked the re-establishment of Achaemenid hegemony over the eastern Aegean.

v.t: Duke Jian of Qin introduces the 'xiangwu' mutual responsibility system
v.s: To strengthen centralization and military mobilization, Duke Jian of Qin issued a decree reorganizing the population: five families formed a wu, ten a shi, with mutual surveillance and collective punishment. Each family registered its population, land and property as basis for taxation and conscription. This system completely replaced the old clan-based management, enabling the state to reach directly into every household. Thereafter Qin could more effectively collect taxes, levy labor and recruit soldiers, laying the foundation for the Shang Yang reforms half a century later.

v.t: Construction of the Apadana palace at Persepolis continues
v.s: Persian King Artaxerxes II continued building the great audience hall 'Apadana' at Persepolis. The hall was supported by thirty-six stone columns with bull and griffin capitals, and its walls bore reliefs of twenty-three tribute-bearing delegations, showing the empire's diverse peoples including Greeks, Egyptians and Indians. The building became a symbol of Achaemenid centralization and multicultural integration.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Socrates sentenced to death by Athenian jury
v.s: The ancient Greek philosopher Socrates was prosecuted by the Athenian democracy on charges of 'not believing in the city's gods' and 'corrupting the youth'. The accusers were the poet Meletus, the politician Anytus and the orator Lycon. A jury of 501 citizens found him guilty by 281 to 220. Socrates refused to recant and also rejected his friends' plans to escape, calmly drinking hemlock. His death made his disciple Plato lose faith in democracy, leading later to the idea of the 'philosopher-king' in the Republic, a foundational text of Western political philosophy.

v.t: Xenophon completes the Anabasis
v.s: The Greek historian Xenophon, while in exile in Sparta, wrote the Anabasis (The Persian Expedition), recounting the harrowing journey of ten thousand Greek mercenaries from Babylon deep in Persia northwards across the Armenian mountains to Trapezus on the Black Sea in 401-399 BC. The book details the mercenaries' organization, tactics, negotiations, and the customs of Persians and Armenian tribes, becoming a primary source for the study of Persian satrapies and Greek military history.

v.t: Pharaoh Amyrtaeus of Egypt's 28th Dynasty defeated and killed
v.s: After Egypt threw off Persian rule, Pharaoh Amyrtaeus of the 28th Dynasty tried to consolidate power in Lower Egypt but was defeated and killed by the general Nepherites I of Mendes. Nepherites established the 29th Dynasty, moving the capital to Mendes. Egypt again fell into civil war, weakening its ability to resist a Persian reconquest.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Syracusan tyrant Dionysius I conquers eastern Sicily
v.s: Taking advantage of a Carthaginian withdrawal due to rebellion in Africa, Dionysius I of Syracuse led an army of Greek mercenaries in a lightning campaign capturing Naxos, Catana and Leontini in eastern Sicily. He built the two-kilometer-long Euryalus fortress to defend Syracuse to the northwest and created a fleet of three hundred ships, many of them quinqueremes. This expansion made Syracuse the strongest Greek city-state on Sicily and prepared it for future hegemony in the western Mediterranean against Carthage.

v.t: Plato leaves Athens for a twelve-year journey
v.s: Disillusioned with Athenian democracy by the death of Socrates, Plato left Athens and traveled to Megara, Cyrene (modern Libya), Egypt and Taranto in southern Italy. In Egypt he learned astronomy and geometry from priests, discovering that Egyptians applied mathematics to land measurement and temple architecture. In Taranto he encountered Pythagorean number philosophy, becoming convinced that the essence of the world is number. These experiences profoundly influenced his later theories of the philosopher-king and the Analogy of the Line in the Republic.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Rome begins the siege of the Etruscan city of Veii
v.s: The war between the Roman Republic and the powerful Etruscan city of Veii had lasted nearly a decade. This year, under the dictator Camillus, Roman forces began a systematic siege of Veii. They dug a tunnel through the city's foundation to cut off its water supply and built siege towers and ram-like battering rams. Because plebeian soldiers had to serve long campaigns letting their farmland go fallow, the Senate was forced to promise that after victory the land of Veii would be distributed to each soldier as compensation.

v.t: Spartan king Agesilaus II ravages Ionia
v.s: Spartan king Agesilaus II led 13,000 Spartan and Greek mercenary troops on a large-scale raid into Ionia, the satrapy of the Persian satrap Tissaphernes, to punish Persia for supporting Athens. He captured Persian strongholds such as Dascylium but, lacking siege engines, could not take major cities like Sardis. The expedition demonstrated the fighting power of the Spartan army but exhausted Sparta's limited treasury.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Rome captures and destroys Veii
v.s: After ten months of tunnel-digging and ground assaults, the Roman dictator Camillus led his troops through the tunnel into Veii, completely capturing the strongest city of the Etruscan League. The Romans sold the adult males into slavery, made the women and children servants of Roman citizens, razed the buildings to the ground and made the land public. This was the first conquest of a major Etruscan city by the Roman Republic, nearly doubling its territory. The plebeians demanded immediate distribution of the booty and land, triggering a decade-long struggle between patricians and plebeians.

v.t: Carthaginian expedition in Sicily suffers disastrous defeat
v.s: Carthage sent Himilco with 100,000 troops and 400 warships to Sicily in an attempt to take Syracuse. But plague ravaged the army, and the Syracusan tyrant Dionysius I attacked from sea and land. Himilco was forced to sign a humiliating treaty with Dionysius, paying 1,000 talents of silver and surrendering most of Sicily. Carthage was unable to advance west for the next twenty years.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Outbreak of the Corinthian War
v.s: With Persian gold and fleet support, Thebes, Athens, Corinth and Argos formed an anti-Spartan alliance, and the Corinthian War officially began. Persia's aim was to weaken Spartan hegemony in Greece in order to recover the Greek cities of Asia Minor. The allied forces killed the famous Spartan commander Lysander at the Battle of Haliartus, but Sparta soon won the Battle of Nemea, leading to a stalemate. The war turned central Greece into a wasteland, and Persia regained control over the Greek cities of Asia Minor.

v.t: Duke Hui of Qin holds a national assembly establishing the prototype of a merit-based military rank system
v.s: To boost morale against the threat of Wei, Duke Hui of Qin convened a national assembly at Yong and announced a new system: any soldier who cut off one enemy head would be granted one rank, one qing of land and nine mu of housing; slaves who cut off heads could be freed. This was the earliest systematic military merit rank system in Chinese history, breaking the hereditary aristocratic system and providing a precedent for the later Shang Yang reforms.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Battle of Cnidus and Athens rebuilds the Long Walls
v.s: The Athenian admiral Conon, commanding an Athenian-Phoenician fleet of over 100 ships financed by Persia, met the Spartan fleet off Cnidus. The Spartan navy was annihilated and its commander Peisander killed. Conon then sailed into Piraeus with 50 talents of Persian gold, used to rebuild the Long Walls from Athens to the port that had been demolished by Sparta after the Peloponnesian War. By 393 BC, the walls were rejoined, marking a partial restoration of Athenian sea power.

v.t: Nepherites I establishes the 29th Dynasty in Egypt
v.s: The Egyptian general Nepherites I proclaimed himself pharaoh at Memphis, founding the 29th Dynasty. He immediately began fortifying defenses against a potential Persian invasion, while also allying with Sparta, providing grain and warships in exchange for Spartan pressure on Persia. Egypt briefly enjoyed independence, but internal struggles for the throne intensified, setting the stage for the turmoil of the 30th Dynasty.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Conon rebuilds the fortifications of Piraeus
v.s: The Persian admiral Conon sailed to Athens a second time, bringing large sums of Persian money to rebuild the walls and ship sheds of Piraeus. The construction used square limestone blocks with walls three meters thick at the base, topped with firing loopholes and patrol walks. Four hundred dry docks were built, along with ropewalks, sail looms and arsenals. Piraeus once again became the strongest naval base in Greece, arousing Spartan protests but no action.

v.t: Achaemenid Empire suppresses the Carian revolt
v.s: The satrap of Caria, Hecatomnus, launched a revolt seeking independence. Persian king Artaxerxes II sent troops to suppress it, and Hecatomnus was defeated and killed. The king appointed his son Mausolus as the new satrap, beginning the Hecatomnid dynasty's hereditary rule over Caria. Mausolus later built the famous Mausoleum of Halicarnassus, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Athens and Sparta hold peace negotiations
v.s: With the Corinthian War at a stalemate, peace talks were held at Sparta. Athens proposed keeping its newly rebuilt Long Walls and a navy of thirty ships, while Sparta insisted on complete demolition of the walls and dissolution of the navy, returning to the state of surrender in 404 BC. The negotiations broke down over the issue of Sparta's garrison in Thebes, but the two sides reached an informal truce suspending major operations for a year. This was the first major diplomatic attempt during the Corinthian War.

v.t: Plato writes the Apology
v.s: Plato began writing the Apology in Athens, a dialogue recording Socrates' self-defense at his trial. In it Socrates calls himself 'a gadfly stinging the lazy horse of Athens', preferring death to giving up philosophy. The Apology became a classic text for understanding Socrates' thought and the Athenian judicial system.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Plato sails to Syracuse for the first time
v.s: Plato accepted an invitation from the Syracusan tyrant Dionysius I and sailed to Sicily, hoping to put his ideal of the philosopher-king into practice. But Dionysius was ill-tempered and valued force over philosophy, leading to a violent quarrel. In anger, Dionysius handed Plato over to a Spartan envoy, who sold him into slavery on the island of Aegina. He was ransomed by his friend Anniceris. This failure convinced Plato that ideal politics could hardly be realized in reality.

v.t: Rome and the Latin League renew the Treaty of Cassius
v.s: Rome and the Latin League renewed the Treaty of Cassius, confirming their military alliance: the Latin towns provided troops and Rome commanded. Rome recognized the internal autonomy of the Latin towns but retained the right to declare war and make treaties. This system became a template for Rome's later control over Italian allies.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Gauls sack Rome
v.s: Brennus, chieftain of the Gallic Senones, led about 40,000 tribesmen across the Apennines, defeated the Roman army thoroughly at the Allia, then marched into Rome unopposed. Except for the temple on the Capitoline Hill, the city was burned to the ground and the senators were massacred. The Gauls besieged the Capitoline for seven months. The defenders starved and finally agreed to pay 1,000 pounds of gold as ransom. While weighing the gold, Brennus used heavier false weights, but the Roman commander Camillus arrived with reinforcements and drove off the Gauls. Rome rebuilt the Servian Wall and undertook military reforms.

v.t: Chu moves its capital to Ying (Jinan)
v.s: To escape the threat of the Three Jins from the north, King Dao of Chu moved the capital from Ying (Yong) to Jinan (modern Jingzhou, Hubei) on the Yangtze. The new capital had palaces, terraces, workshops and beacon towers, with walls 15 km in circumference, becoming the largest southern metropolis of the Warring States period. Chu thus strengthened its control over the middle Yangtze and began contending with Ba and Shu for the southwest.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Gauls continue ravaging Etruria
v.s: After sacking Rome and receiving the ransom, Brennus led his Gallic army south into Etruria, systematically looting the cities of Caere, Tarquinia and Volsinii. The Etruscans could not resist and had to pay large amounts of grain, olive oil and bronze as tribute to save themselves. The Roman consul Camillus organized an Etruscan league to expel the Gauls, but the cities distrusted each other and failed. The Gauls rampaged through central Italy for two years before returning home laden with booty.

v.t: Wu Qi trains the Wei Wuzu elite troops
v.s: Marquis Wen of Wei appointed Wu Qi as general. In the Hexi region, Wu Qi selected 50,000 tall, strong soldiers, outfitting them with three layers of armor, carrying crossbows and halberds, and making them march 50 li carrying heavy loads. This elite force, called the Wei Wuzu, each had two horses and an ox, and their pay and treatment far exceeded that of ordinary soldiers. Wu Qi shared the soldiers' hardships and sucked pus from wounds, making the Wei Wuzu the strongest infantry force of the early Warring States.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Plato founds the Academy in northwestern Athens
v.s: Plato bought an olive grove in the grove of Academus northwest of Athens, establishing the first institution of higher learning in the West. The Academy focused on mathematics and philosophy, requiring ten years of geometry before admission, with the motto 'Let no one ignorant of geometry enter here' over the gate. His students included Aristotle, Speusippus and Xenocrates. The Academy operated for 906 years until closed by the Byzantine emperor Justinian in 529 AD.

v.t: Democritus completes his atomic theory
v.s: The ancient Greek philosopher Democritus completed works such as the Great World System in Abdera, systematically expounding atomism: the universe consists of indivisible atoms and void; differences in atomic shape, size and arrangement account for all differences among things. He also proposed that 'soul atoms' are spherical and most active, explaining perception and thought. Atomism became the origin of later scientific materialism.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Peace of Antalcidas (King's Peace) signed
v.s: Sparta and Persia signed the King's Peace at Susa: Sparta fully recognized Persian ownership of the Greek cities of Asia Minor and Cyprus, while Persia forced all Greek city-states, including Athens and Thebes, to accept Sparta as the hegemon of Greece. The treaty stipulated that all Greek alliances except the Peloponnesian League were to be dissolved, and Thebes was forced to dissolve the Boeotian League. This treaty gave Persia legitimate power to interfere in Greek affairs, and although Sparta appeared victorious, it became Persia's vassal in Greece.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Tian replaces Jiang: King An of Zhou enfeoffs Tian He as Marquis of Qi
v.s: The Tian clan of Qi had controlled the government for generations. Tian He sent envoys with lavish gifts to Wei, and through Marquis Wen of Wei requested investiture from King An of Zhou. The king, unable to refuse, formally enfeoffed Tian He as Marquis of Qi, ending the Jiang lineage of Qi. The Tian clan, learning from the Jiang's loss of popular support, immediately halved taxes, patronized the Jixia Academy, and opened border markets to attract merchants, quickly restoring Qi's power.

v.t: Spartan king Agesilaus II campaigns against Mantinea
v.s: Spartan king Agesilaus II led the Peloponnesian League army against Mantinea, accusing it of violating the King's Peace and harbouring Spartan exiles. After a months-long siege, Mantinea surrendered, and Sparta forced it to demolish its walls and disperse its inhabitants into four villages. This was the last successful military operation by Sparta to maintain its Peloponnesian hegemony.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Demosthenes born in Athens
v.s: The greatest orator of ancient Greece, Demosthenes, was born into a wealthy Athenian family, his father a sword-maker. His father died when he was seven, and his inheritance was embezzled by guardians. To reclaim his property, he taught himself law and overcame a congenital stutter by speaking with pebbles in his mouth, reciting poetry on the beach, and declaiming while running. He became a professional speechwriter and later delivered the Philippics to lead Athens in resisting Macedonian expansion.

v.t: Plato visits Taranto for the first time
v.s: During a break from teaching at the Academy, Plato sailed to Taranto in southern Italy to visit the Pythagorean leader Archytas. They discussed the relationship between mathematics and philosophy, and Archytas demonstrated a mechanical dove – possibly the earliest artificial flying device. This visit deepened Plato's understanding of the Pythagorean idea that 'all is number'.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Aristotle born in Stagira, Thrace
v.s: Aristotle was born in the small border town of Stagira in the kingdom of Macedon. His father Nicomachus was the court physician to King Amyntas III of Macedon. From childhood he was exposed to the biological and medical knowledge of the Macedonian court, developing habits of observation and classification. At seventeen he went to Athens and entered Plato's Academy, where Plato called him 'the mind of the school'.

v.t: Theban coup overthrows pro-Spartan oligarchs
v.s: Theban democrats led by Pelopidas and Epaminondas, taking advantage of the winter lull in the Spartan garrison, launched an uprising and killed the Spartan-installed oligarchic rulers. Thebes announced the restoration of the Boeotian League. Sparta sent troops to suppress it, but with Athenian support the Thebans held out for two years, setting the stage for the later Battle of Leuctra.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Reconstitution of the Boeotian League
v.s: Thebes formally organized a new Boeotian League comprising eleven cities including Thebes, Coronea, Orchomenus and Thespiae. The league was federal: each member contributed troops and taxes in proportion to its population, and Thebes provided two of the seven generals. Sparta declared this a violation of the King's Peace and began preparing to march against Thebes.

v.t: Athens introduces pay for public office
v.s: The Athenian Assembly passed a law granting daily pay of two obols (about half a day's labor wage) to jurors and members of the Council. The measure, proposed by the democratic leader Argyrios, aimed to enable poor citizens to participate in politics. However, it also increased the fiscal burden, forcing Athens to tighten tribute collection from its allies.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Sparta seizes the Cadmea, the acropolis of Thebes
v.s: The Spartan general Phoebidas, marching his army past Thebes, took advantage of infighting between democratic and oligarchic factions to seize the undefended Cadmea. Spartan king Agesilaus II refused to punish Phoebidas and instead endorsed his action. Sparta established a pro-Spartan oligarchy in Thebes and exiled 300 democrats including Pelopidas. This treacherous act aroused anger among Greek cities, but no one dared openly resist.

v.t: Maya civilization begins construction of Uxmal pyramids
v.s: Around 382 BC, early settlements at Uxmal on the Yucatán Peninsula began to form during the Maya development. However the most magnificent buildings of Uxmal – the Governor's Palace, the Pyramid of the Magician and the Nunnery Quadrangle – were built in the Late Classic period (c. AD 600-900). In 382 BC, the Maya were in the Middle Preclassic, with developing cities such as El Mirador.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Sparta suppresses a Phocian revolt
v.s: Phocis, a city-state in central Greece, resented Spartan control over the management and offerings of the Delphic temple and rose in rebellion. Spartan king Agesilaus II led a Peloponnesian army into Phocis, burning twelve villages and plundering the animals and precious vessels of the temple. Phocis was forced to resubmit, and Sparta gave control of Delphi to a pro-Spartan ally.

v.t: Euclid born in Athens
v.s: The ancient Greek mathematician Euclid was born in Athens. Later, under the patronage of Ptolemy I, he went to teach at the Musaeum of Alexandria, where he wrote the Elements, systematically organizing the geometry of his time into thirteen books, deducing 467 propositions from five postulates. The Elements became the most widely circulated textbook in the history of science.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Plato completes the Republic
v.s: Plato finished his masterwork the Republic at the Academy. The book, with Socrates as its main speaker, explores 'what is justice' and constructs an ideal city ruled by a philosopher-king. It presents the Allegory of the Cave, the Simile of the Sun, and the Divided Line, arguing for the separation of the world of Forms from the sensible world, and discusses radical proposals such as equality of women, communal living and the abolition of private property.

v.t: Isocrates delivers the Panegyricus
v.s: The Athenian rhetorician Isocrates delivered a speech at the Olympic Games calling on all Greek city-states to stop fighting each other and unite in a campaign against Persia. He proposed that Athens supply the navy, Sparta the army, and that the war be taken to Asia, using Persian wealth to revive Greece. The speech aroused much discussion but was not adopted by any city.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Theban uprising expels the Spartan garrison
v.s: Theban democratic exiles in Athens, led by Pelopidas and Epaminondas, sneaked back into Thebes disguised as hunters with eleven comrades. At night they killed the oligarchic leaders and roused the citizens to attack the Cadmea. Athens sent 5,000 hoplites in support, and the Spartan garrison surrendered from starvation. Thebes restored democracy, the Boeotian League was re-formed, and Epaminondas began military reforms.

v.t: Lost-wax casting of Chinese bronzes reaches maturity
v.s: The pan and zun vessels from the tomb of Marquis Yi of Zeng in Suizhou, Hubei, demonstrate the high maturity of the lost-wax casting process. Craftsmen first made a wax model, coated it with clay, then heated it to melt the wax before pouring molten bronze, enabling intricate openwork and coiled serpent patterns. This technique was later used for cloud-pattern bronze vessels and Warring States tripods.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Athens forms the Second Athenian League
v.s: Athens, together with Thebes, Ceos, Mytilene and over sixty other city-states, signed an alliance in Athens establishing the Second Athenian League. The treaty expressly stated that Athens would station no garrisons and exact no tribute from allied states; all members had equal voting rights in the assembly; and disputes among allies would be settled by an allied court. This was an attempt to correct the abuses of the First Delian League, but the foundation of the new league was still fragile.

v.t: Shang Yang born in the state of Wei
v.s: The Legalist reformer Shang Yang (Gongsun Yang) was born into an aristocratic family in the state of Wei. In his youth he studied the reform ideas of Li Kui and Wu Qi. Later he served as a minor official under the Wei prime minister Gongshu Cuo, who on his deathbed recommended him to King Hui of Wei, but the king did not use him. Hearing that Duke Xiao of Qin had issued a call for talent, Shang Yang took the Canon of Laws and went west to Qin, launching the famous reforms.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Sparta invades Boeotia
v.s: Spartan king Agesilaus II led 20,000 infantry and 1,000 cavalry into Boeotia, aiming to destroy the Thebes-led Boeotian League. The Theban general Epaminondas used new tactics, stationing the elite Sacred Band on the left wing. But when the two armies met at Thespiae, heavy rain and mud prevented a decisive battle. Sparta plundered the countryside and withdrew. This was the last large-scale Spartan invasion of central Greece.

v.t: Construction of the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus begins
v.s: Mausolus, satrap of Caria in the Persian Empire, began building a tomb for himself at his capital Halicarnassus (modern Bodrum, Turkey). The project brought together Greek, Egyptian and Persian architects. The base was a square podium, surrounded by thirty-six Ionic columns, topped with a pyramidal roof, and crowned by a statue of a four-horse chariot. This tomb would later be called the Mausoleum of Halicarnassus and listed among the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Battle of Naxos: Athens defeats Spartan fleet
v.s: The Athenian admiral Chabrias, with eighty triremes, met sixty Spartan triremes off Naxos. Athens used a concentration tactic, breaking the Spartan center with ten fast ships then enveloping both wings. Sparta lost forty-nine ships, Athens only eighteen. This was Athens' first naval victory over Sparta since the Peloponnesian War, restoring Athenian control over Aegean sea lanes and bringing dozens of city-states into the Second Athenian League.

v.t: Wu Qi's reforms in Chu fail, and he is killed
v.s: King Dao of Chu appointed Wu Qi to carry out reforms: abolishing aristocratic privileges, reducing stipends, and moving nobles with no office for three generations to frontier regions to open wasteland. After King Dao's death, the old nobles rose in rebellion and shot Wu Qi. He threw himself onto the king's corpse, and the arrows hit the dead king's body as well. The new king, King Su of Chu, used this as a pretext to execute over seventy noble families, but most of the reform measures were abolished, leaving only the household registration system and military ranks.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Theban Sacred Band defeats Spartans at Thespiae
v.s: Pelopidas led the three-hundred-strong Theban Sacred Band, consisting of 150 male couples, against two Spartan battalions at Thespiae. The Sacred Band fought to the death to protect their lovers, broke the Spartan hoplites with a wedge formation, and captured three Spartan officers. This was the first time a Theban army had defeated Spartan forces in open battle, breaking the myth of Spartan hoplite invincibility.

v.t: Large-scale mining resumes at Athens' Laurion silver mines
v.s: The Laurion silver mines of Athens, after lying idle since the Peloponnesian War, resumed large-scale extraction. Miners used new techniques of tunnel excavation and hydraulic washing, producing about twenty tons of silver annually. The mines were operated with slave labor and contract farming, with half the proceeds going to the treasury and half to the contractors. Trade at Piraeus returned to pre-war levels, and exports of pottery, wine and olive oil continued to grow.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Athens and Sparta sign a five-year truce
v.s: At the mediation of Persian king Artaxerxes II, Athens and Sparta signed a five-year truce at Sparta. Each retained its existing territories; Athens recognized Sparta's control over the Peloponnese; Sparta acquiesced in the existence of the Second Athenian League. Thebes was not invited to sign and announced it would continue to resist Sparta.

v.t: Greek comedy playwright Menander born
v.s: Menander, the leading poet of New Comedy, was born in Athens. He studied under Theophrastus and wrote over 108 plays. His surviving works, such as Dyskolos and Samia, depict ordinary urban life, abandoning the political and personal satire of Old Comedy. Menander's plays were lost in the Middle Ages and only recovered in the twentieth century from papyrus finds.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Earthquake and tsunami destroy the ancient Greek city of Helike
v.s: A strong earthquake in the Gulf of Corinth triggered a 15-meter-high tsunami. Helike, the richest city in Achaea, was swallowed by the sea overnight. The temple of Hera, the marketplace and shipyards all sank, leaving no survivors. The ancient geographer Pausanias later recorded that animals had fled to higher ground before the quake. This 'Greek Atlantis' was only rediscovered by archaeologists on the seabed in 2001.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Athens and Sparta resume war
v.s: Sparta supported an oligarchic revolt in Athens' ally Cephallenia, which attacked the Athenian garrison. The Athenian assembly voted to end the truce, sending Timotheus with sixty ships to besiege Sparta-controlled Zakynthos. Four naval battles were fought in the Ionian Sea with mixed results, and war in Greece fully resumed.

v.t: Mencius born in the state of Zou
v.s: The Confucian thinker Mencius (Meng Ke) was born in Zou (modern Zoucheng, Shandong). He studied under a disciple of Zisi and developed Confucius' teachings. He proposed the theory of the innate goodness of human nature, arguing that humans are born with four 'sprouts': compassion, shame, deference, and right and wrong. He traveled to courts such as Qi and Liang, advocating benevolent government and the way of the king, opposing hegemonism and annexation wars. Although not employed in his time, he was later honored as the 'Second Sage'.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Battle of Leuctra: Thebes breaks Spartan hegemony
v.s: The Theban general Epaminondas led 6,000 troops (including the 300-strong Sacred Band) at Leuctra against the Spartan king Cleombrotus with 11,000 men. Epaminondas massed his elite forces on the left wing in a wedge formation fifty ranks deep and used an oblique tactic to break through the Spartan right, killing Cleombrotus on the spot. Sparta lost 1,700 of its citizen soldiers, Thebes only 300. This battle ended three centuries of Spartan military hegemony, and Thebes became the new hegemon of Greece.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Thebes invades the Peloponnese and establishes the state of Messenia
v.s: Epaminondas led a Peloponnesian army of 70,000 (including Argos, Mantinea and some helots) southward, liberating the Messenian helots who had been enslaved by Sparta for three hundred years. He built a new Messenian capital on Mount Ithome, with walls 9 km in circumference, and invited all exiled Messenians to return. Sparta thereby lost half its territory and eighty percent of its agricultural population, and was reduced to a second-rate power.

v.t: Xenophon writes the Hellenica
v.s: The Greek historian Xenophon wrote the Hellenica, continuing the narrative of Thucydides' History of the Peloponnesian War, covering Greek history from 411 to 362 BC. It details the rise and fall of Spartan hegemony, the rise of Thebes and Persian intervention in Greece, and is the most important primary source for fourth-century Greek history.

d.u: King An of Zhou, Ji Jiao

v.t: Athens and Sparta ally against Thebes
v.s: Fearing the rise of Thebes, Athens and Sparta set aside their differences and concluded a military alliance. Athens sent 5,000 hoplites and twenty ships to aid Sparta, and both agreed not to make a separate peace with Thebes. This was the only formal alliance between Athens and Sparta in Greek history, but mutual distrust prevented effective cooperation.

v.t: Zhuangzi born in Meng, state of Song
v.s: The Daoist thinker Zhuangzi (Zhuang Zhou) was born in Meng, state of Song (modern Shangqiu, Henan or Mengcheng, Anhui). He inherited and developed Laozi's Daoist thought, advocating the Way of nature and non-action, expressing spiritual freedom through fables. In the 'Free and Easy Wandering' he proposes the state of 'no dependence', and in 'Discussion on Making All Things Equal' he argues for the equality of all things. Zhuangzi declined an offer from King Wei of Chu to become prime minister, remaining an office hermit all his life.

d.u: King Lie of Zhou, Ji Xi

v.t: Macedonian king Alexander II assassinated
v.s: King Alexander II of Macedon was assassinated by his brother-in-law Ptolemy at his sister's wedding banquet. His brother Perdiccas III was still a child, and Ptolemy assumed the regency. The queen mother Eurydice requested the intervention of the Theban general Pelopidas, who defeated Ptolemy and took Perdiccas III to Thebes as a hostage, while bringing the young Philip (later Philip II) back to Macedon. Philip learned Epaminondas' military tactics in Thebes.

v.t: King Wei of Qi improves administration, Qi becomes strong
v.s: After ascending the throne, King Wei of Qi summoned the magistrates of Jimo and A to personally assess their performance. He lavishly rewarded the magistrate of Jimo and boiled alive the magistrate of A and his accomplices. The state of Qi was shaken. King Wei then appointed Zou Ji as chancellor, who revised the laws, supervised officials, and opened markets to attract merchants. Qi gradually became a powerful state in the east.

d.u: King Lie of Zhou, Ji Xi

v.t: Rome passes the Licinio-Sextian laws
v.s: The plebeian tribunes Gaius Licinius and Lucius Sextius proposed three laws: all interest already paid on debts be deducted from the principal, with the remainder to be paid in three years; no one could hold more than 500 iugera of public land; one of the two consuls must be a plebeian. After ten years of struggle and several appointments of dictators, the patrician assembly finally passed the laws. In 366 BC, Sextius was elected the first plebeian consul.

d.u: King Lie of Zhou, Ji Xi

v.t: Epaminondas's third Peloponnesian campaign
v.s: The Theban commander Epaminondas led 20,000 allied troops south into the Peloponnese for the third time, besieging Phlius, Sparta's last remaining ally. Spartan king Agesilaus II came to the relief with 3,000 troops, and the two armies faced each other for thirty days. Due to supply difficulties, Thebes withdrew, but Sparta was no longer able to prevent Theban influence from spreading throughout the Peloponnese.

v.t: Persian Empire suppresses the revolt of western satraps
v.s: Ariobarzanes, satrap of western Asia Minor, rebelled and proclaimed himself king. Persian king Artaxerxes II sent forces to suppress him, and after two years of war, took Assos and executed Ariobarzanes. Persia regained control of Ionia and the Hellespont, but its power was greatly weakened.

d.u: King Lie of Zhou, Ji Xi

v.t: Li Kui of Wei publishes the Canon of Laws
v.s: Marquis Wen of Wei appointed Li Kui to carry out reforms. Li Kui compiled the criminal codes of various states into the Canon of Laws (Fajing), divided into six chapters: theft, robbery, arrest, detention, miscellaneous, and general. It covered theft, murder, arrest, trial, and other legal procedures. The Canon of Laws is China's first relatively systematic written code, abolishing the principle of 'punishment not known, therefore the power is not seen'. Li Kui also promoted the 'Doctrine of Exhausting Land Power', raising grain yields to three shi per mu, and implemented the 'price stabilization method' to prevent prices from harming farmers.

v.t: Second Athenian League begins to collapse
v.s: Athens imposed tribute and stationed garrisons on Delos, Euboea and other allies, violating the league's charter. Chios, Rhodes, Cos and Byzantium successively seceded and turned to Persia for protection. The Athenian admiral Chares led fifty ships to suppress them, starting the Social War.

d.u: King Lie of Zhou, Ji Xi

v.t: Pelopidas falls at the Battle of Cynoscephalae
v.s: The Theban general Pelopidas, while pursuing the Thessalian tyrant Alexander, was ambushed in the hills of Cynoscephalae. Pelopidas charged personally into the enemy ranks, killing several before being pierced through the chest by a spear and dying. The Theban army withdrew in grief. The Thessalian tyrant was forced to return Pelopidas' body to Thebes along with a letter of submission. Thebes gave him a state funeral.

v.t: Carthaginian expedition to Sicily fails, signs peace with Syracuse
v.s: Carthage again sent an expedition to Sicily, but at the Battle of Cabala, the Syracusan tyrant Dionysius I surrounded them, killing 15,000 men. Carthage was forced to sue for peace, ceding all of western Sicily and paying 3,000 talents of silver. Syracuse became the sole hegemon of Sicily.

d.u: King Lie of Zhou, Ji Xi

v.t: Philip II reforms the Macedonian army
v.s: Philip II, who had been a hostage in Thebes, learned Epaminondas' tactics and military organization. After returning to Macedon, he was appointed regent by his brother King Perdiccas III. He began reforming the Macedonian army: infantry were formed into a phalanx sixteen ranks deep, each man armed with a 6-meter sarissa; heavy cavalry (the Companions) were established as a shock force; and he invented mobile siege towers and torsion catapults. The military power of Macedon began to transform.

d.u: King Lie of Zhou, Ji Xi

v.t: Battle of Mantinea and death of Epaminondas
v.s: Four armies – Thebes, Sparta, Athens and Mantinea – met on the plain of Mantinea. Epaminondas again used the Leuctra tactic, concentrating his elite on the left wing and breaking the Spartan right, but while pursuing, he was struck in the chest by a Spartan javelin and died three days later. Thebes won the battle but lost its greatest general, and its hegemony rapidly declined. After this battle, all Greek states were exhausted, paving the way for the rise of Macedon.

d.u: King Lie of Zhou, Ji Xi

v.t: Duke Xiao of Qin issues an order seeking talent
v.s: The 21-year-old Duke Xiao of Qin, distressed that Qin had lost the Hexi region to Wei and that its eastern exit through Hangu Pass was blocked, issued a call for talent at the capital Yueyang, promising: 'If any guest or minister can devise a brilliant plan to strengthen Qin, I will give him high office and share the land with him.' Shang Yang (Gongsun Yang) from Wei, carrying the Canon of Laws, went west to Qin. Through three audiences arranged by the eunuch Jing Jian, he finally persuaded Duke Xiao with his doctrine of state power, launching the most thorough reform in Chinese history.

v.t: Athens, Sparta and Thebes sign a Common Peace
v.s: The Greek states convened a peace conference at Thebes, agreeing that all cities should be independent and no city should station troops in another. Athens acknowledged the dissolution of the Second Athenian League, Sparta accepted the independence of Messenia, and Thebes gave up its leadership of the Boeotian League. This Common Peace maintained a brief four years of peace but could not stop the rise of Macedon.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Shang Yang issues Qin's first reform decrees
v.s: Duke Xiao of Qin appointed Shang Yang as Chief of the Left and carried out the first round of reforms: household registration with mutual surveillance in groups of five; military ranks based on beheading enemies, with hereditary aristocratic privilege abolished; rewards for farming and weaving – those producing much grain and cloth could be exempted from corvée; severe punishment for private feuding, with penalties according to the gravity. The reforms initially met opposition from the old aristocracy, and when the crown prince violated the law, his tutor, Prince Qian, suffered nose-cutting. But after a year, the people of Qin gradually accepted the new laws.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II formally ascends as king of Macedon
v.s: The Macedonian king Perdiccas III fell in battle against the Illyrians. Philip II, returning from Thebes, was hailed as king by the army at the age of twenty-three. He immediately reformed the army, subdued the western Lyncestian tribes, and opened gold mines on Mount Pangaeum, producing 1,000 talents annually. He issued the gold Philippus coin, which became an international medium of exchange, swiftly strengthening Macedonia.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II conquers Paeonia
v.s: Philip II led 20,000 troops north against Paeonia. At the Battle of the Lychnius valley, he defeated King Agis of Paeonia using phalanx breakthrough tactics. Paeonia was forced to become a Macedonian vassal, providing 600 cavalry and 50 talents of silver annually. Macedonia's northern frontier secured, Philip could now concentrate on the Illyrians.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II captures Amphipolis
v.s: Taking advantage of a war between Athens and its allies, Philip II led 30,000 troops to besiege the important city of Amphipolis at the mouth of the Strymon. Athens had promised to cede it to Athens if taken, but after capturing it Philip refused to hand it over and instead opened war with Athens. Amphipolis controlled the gold mines of Mount Pangaeum and the forests providing ship timber, giving Macedonia a coveted outlet to the sea.

v.t: Outbreak of the Social War
v.s: Chios, Rhodes, Cos and Byzantium, resentful of Athens' tribute collection and garrisoning, withdrew from the Second Athenian League and formed an 'Alliance of Allies' to resist Athens. Athens sent Chares with sixty ships to suppress them, and a three-year naval war in the Aegean began.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Shang Yang's second reforms and Qin moves capital to Xianyang
v.s: Shang Yang carried out the second round of reforms: abolition of the well-field system, permitting land sales; standardization of weights and measures with official measuring vessels; introduction of prefectures (31 counties), with prefects appointed by the ruler; encouragement of family division – double taxation for families with two adult sons living together. Qin moved its capital from Yueyang to Xianyang, building palaces, gates and walls. The old aristocrat Prince Qian again violated the law and suffered nose-cutting, allowing the new laws to be fully implemented.

v.t: Alexander born in Pella
v.s: Philip II and his Epirote wife Olympias gave birth to Alexander at the old Macedonian capital Pella. According to Plutarch, Olympias dreamed of a thunderbolt striking her womb, signifying her son's extraordinary destiny. Philip II hired Aristotle as his tutor. Alexander later became the greatest conqueror of the ancient world.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Social War ends; Athens admits defeat
v.s: The Athenian fleet was defeated by the Allies' fleet off Byzantium, losing thirty ships and exhausting the treasury. Athens was forced to sign a peace recognizing the independence of Chios, Rhodes, Cos and Byzantium, and abandoning its right to collect tolls in the Hellespont. The Second Athenian League effectively dissolved, and Athens retreated to Attica, becoming a second-rate power.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II besieges Methone, loses an eye
v.s: Philip II led his army to besiege the coastal Macedonian city of Methone. During an assault, a bolt from a ballista fired by a defender pierced Philip's right eye, damaging the eyeball. The army surgeon removed the eye but kept the arrowhead as a souvenir. Philip continued to command in agony, eventually taking the city. He treated the prisoners leniently, only exiling the resistors, but forbade anyone to mention the word 'one-eyed' in his presence.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Third Sacred War breaks out
v.s: The Phocians occupied the temple of Apollo at Delphi and looted the vast wealth accumulated there over nearly a century, including gold and silver vessels and votive offerings, totalling over 10,000 talents. They used this wealth to hire a 20,000-strong mercenary army. The Amphictyonic League, led by Thebes, declared war on Phocis. Sparta and Athens supported Phocis, while Thebes and Macedon supported the League. Philip II intervened in Greek affairs under the pretext of protecting the temple.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II defeats Phocians at the Battle of Crocus Field
v.s: Philip II led 20,000 Macedonian phalangites and 2,000 cavalry against the Phocian general Onomarchus with 30,000 mercenaries at the Crocus Field in Thessaly. The Macedonian phalanx, with its six-meter pikes forming a wall of iron, shattered the Phocian army, and Onomarchus fell. Philip had 6,000 captives drowned as punishment. The Thessalian League elected him its supreme commander. This was the first time Macedonia had gained a military voice in central Greece.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Demosthenes delivers the First Philippic
v.s: The Athenian orator Demosthenes addressed the Assembly, warning that Macedonian expansion threatened all of Greece. He criticized Athenians for indulging in the comfort of theoric subsidies and urged immediate dispatch of troops to support the Thracian cities, and the conversion of the citizen militia into a standing army. His speech did not lead to immediate action but began to focus Athenian attention on the northern threat.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Chu lacquerware reaches its peak
v.s: Lacquerware from the Chu tombs at Wangshan, Jiangling, Hubei, dates to this period, including tiger-and-phoenix drums, painted wood screens and cloud-patterned ear cups. Black lacquer was used as the base, with red, yellow and gold designs of clouds, phoenixes and hunting scenes. Wood carving is extremely intricate. The state of Chu established workshops to manage lacquer production, and the products were exported to the Central Plain, Ba-Shu and the southwestern barbarian regions.

v.t: Persian Empire suppresses the Cypriot revolt
v.s: Evagoras, king of Salamis on Cyprus, revolted and declared independence from Persia, with naval support from Athens. Persian king Artaxerxes III besieged Salamis for ten months, finally breaking in; Evagoras fell. Persia reconquered Cyprus and made it a satrapy.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II intervenes in central Greek city-states
v.s: At the request of Thebes, Philip II led his army into Phocis and destroyed the remaining Phocian resistance. He transferred Phocis's two votes in the Delphic Amphictyony to Thebes, and also occupied Thermopylae. Athens sent aid to Olynthus in Chalcidice, trying to prevent Macedonian expansion southward.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II captures Olynthus
v.s: The Macedonian army besieged Olynthus with siege towers and battering rams. Athens sent thirty ships to relieve it, but they were delayed by storms. After three months of siege, the walls were breached and Olynthus fell. Philip razed the city and sold its 30,000 inhabitants into slavery. Athens lost its last powerful ally, and the door to central Greece was now wide open for Macedon.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Plato dies in Athens
v.s: At the age of eighty, Plato died peacefully in his sleep after attending a student's wedding. He bequeathed the Academy to his nephew Speusippus. Plato left over thirty dialogues and thirteen letters. His theory of Forms and the concept of the philosopher-king influenced all of Western philosophy. The Academy continued for 900 years, until closed by the Byzantine emperor Justinian in 529 AD.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Peace of Philocrates signed
v.s: Athens sent a ten-man embassy including Demosthenes and Aeschines to Pella to negotiate peace with Philip II. The treaty provided: Athens gave up Amphipolis and Potidaea, recognized Macedonian rule over Thessaly, and the two sides exchanged prisoners. Philip promised not to attack Attica or the Black Sea grain route. The Athenians celebrated peace, with only Demosthenes firmly opposing it as a betrayal of Greece.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Aristotle travels to Asia Minor and marries
v.s: After Plato's death, Aristotle, unable to get along with the new head of the Academy Speusippus, left Athens and accepted an invitation from Hermias to Atarneus in Asia Minor. Hermias gave him his niece Pythias in marriage, and Aristotle established a school at Assos, studying biology and political systems. He later accepted an invitation from Philip II to become tutor to Alexander.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II suppresses Thessalian revolt
v.s: Thessalian nobles, resentful of the Macedonian garrison, rose in rebellion at Pelinna and besieged the Macedonian garrison. Philip II, with light troops, force-marched 100 kilometers in a day, reached the city, and used his sarissa phalanx to defeat the rebels. He established four fortresses in southern Thessaly and declared that the Thessalian League would be loyal to Macedon forever.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Aristotle hired as tutor to the Macedonian prince Alexander
v.s: Philip II invited Aristotle to become tutor to the thirteen-year-old Alexander and his young noble companions. Aristotle taught at the sanctuary of the Nymphs at Mieza, instructing in Homeric epic, politics, ethics and natural science, and specially prepared a commentary on the Iliad for Alexander. Aristotle's education shaped Alexander's love of Greek culture, and he carried the Iliad with him throughout his campaigns.

v.t: First Samnite War breaks out
v.s: Rome and the Samnites went to war over control of Capua and the Campanian plain. The Roman consuls Valerius and Cornelius defeated the Samnites before Capua, and the Samnites were forced to sue for peace, forming an unequal alliance. Rome gained control of Campania, extending its power into southern Italy.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Macedon invades Thrace
v.s: Philip II led 30,000 troops east into Thrace, defeating King Cersobleptes' army at Mount Marmarian and occupying all of Thrace. He founded Philippopolis (modern Plovdiv, Bulgaria) on the Hebrus river, controlling the land route from the Black Sea to the Aegean, and imposed an annual tribute of 500 talents of gold on the Thracians.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Battle of Maling: Qi crushes Wei
v.s: Pang Juan of Wei led 100,000 troops against Han. Qi sent Tian Ji as general and Sun Bin as adviser to save Han. Sun Bin used the 'diminishing stoves' stratagem: on the first day of retreat, they made 100,000 cooking pits, on the second day 50,000, on the third day 30,000. Pang Juan concluded that Qi had lost half its army, abandoned his heavy infantry and pressed ahead with light troops. Sun Bin ambushed him at Maling (modern Shenxian, Shandong). Ten thousand crossbows fired at once, and Pang Juan committed suicide. The Wei crown prince was captured, and nearly all of Wei's 100,000 troops were lost. This battle ended Wei's hegemony, and Qi rose as a powerful eastern state.

v.t: Epicurus born on Samos
v.s: The ancient Greek philosopher Epicurus was born on Samos, the son of an Athenian immigrant. He began studying philosophy at fourteen, went to Athens for military service at eighteen, and later established a school in Asia Minor. He founded the 'Garden' school in Athens, teaching that the goal of life is happiness, which is freedom from bodily pain and mental disturbance. He developed Democritus's atomism. His thought, together with Stoicism, was one of the two major philosophical schools of the Hellenistic age.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II besieges Byzantium
v.s: Philip II, with 160 ships and 30,000 land troops, besieged Byzantium, the key to the Bosporus, in an attempt to cut Athens' Black Sea grain supply line. Athens immediately sent a relief fleet, and the defenders resisted fiercely from their strong walls. The Macedonian forces used siege towers and mines for several months, but Philip was wounded in the leg by a javelin and forced to withdraw. Athens preserved its grain route, but the initiative in Black Sea trade was no longer in Athenian hands.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Fourth Sacred War (War of Amphissa)
v.s: The Locrians of Amphissa cultivated land consecrated to Delphi, and the Amphictyonic League declared war on Amphissa. Thebes and Athens, under the banner of 'defending Greek freedom', supported Amphissa, while Philip II, on the pretext of protecting the temple, marched south and seized Elatea, directly threatening Thebes. The Greek situation became extremely tense, and an anti-Macedonian coalition began to form.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Battle of Chaeronea: Macedon conquers Greece
v.s: Philip II and his eighteen-year-old son Alexander led 30,000 Macedonian troops against 35,000 Athenian-Theban hoplites at Chaeronea. Alexander commanding the left wing cavalry first broke the Theban Sacred Band, while Philip in person drove back the Athenian phalanx. The allied forces were routed: 1,000 Athenians fell, 2,000 were captured; the 300-strong Theban Sacred Band were all killed. Athens and Thebes were forced to accept Macedonian garrisons and pro-Macedonian oligarchies. The independence of the Greek city-states ended.

v.t: Shang Yang executed by dismemberment (che li)
v.s: Duke Xiao of Qin died, and the crown prince Si ascended the throne as King Huiwen of Qin. The old aristocrats, led by Prince Qian, accused Shang Yang of plotting rebellion. Shang Yang fled to Wei, but Wei refused him entry because of his earlier deception of Prince Ang. He returned to Qin and raised troops, but was defeated and killed at Tong. His corpse was carted to Xianyang and torn apart by chariots, and his family was exterminated. However, King Huiwen retained Shang Yang's new laws, and Qin continued to grow strong.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II establishes the League of Corinth
v.s: Philip II convened a congress of all Greek city-states at Corinth (Sparta absent) and established the League of Corinth. Each city-state was to be independent and not to attack each other; no changes of regime were permitted; all states contributed contingents to a joint army for a campaign against Persia, to avenge the Persian Wars; Philip was named supreme commander (hegemon), and any state that seceded would be subject to collective sanctions. The Greek city-states formally became vassals of Macedon.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Philip II assassinated at his daughter's wedding; Alexander succeeds
v.s: Philip II was stabbed to death by his bodyguard Pausanias at the wedding of his daughter Cleopatra to the king of Epirus in the old capital Aegae. The twenty-year-old Alexander quickly suppressed northern rebellions, purged the conspirators, and assumed the throne. He then received from the League of Corinth the command of the Greek forces for the planned invasion of Persia.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Alexander destroys Thebes to terrify Greece
v.s: Thebes, taking advantage of a rumor that Alexander had died, rose in revolt and besieged the Macedonian garrison. Alexander force-marched from Illyria to Thebes in fifteen days, stormed the city, and ordered it razed to the ground, sparing only the temples and the house of the poet Pindar. Thirty thousand Thebans were sold into slavery. The Greek states were shocked and none dared rebel again.

v.t: Aristotle returns to Athens and founds the Lyceum
v.s: Alexander financed Aristotle with 8,000 gold talents (equivalent to hundreds of millions of dollars today). Aristotle established a school at the Lyceum gymnasium in eastern Athens. He taught while walking, hence the school became known as the Peripatetic school. The school included a museum, a library and a zoo, housing biological specimens from all over Greece, covering all fields of knowledge.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Alexander crosses the Hellespont, beginning his eastern campaign
v.s: In spring, Alexander with 30,000 infantry, 5,000 cavalry and 160 warships crossed the Hellespont (Dardanelles). He sacrificed at Troy, honoring Achilles. Then at the Battle of the Granicus, Alexander led the Companion cavalry in the charge, defeating the Persian forces for the first time, killing three Persian satraps, and beginning his ten-year campaign.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Battle of Issus: Alexander routs Darius III
v.s: Persian king Darius III, commanding a purported 600,000 (actual perhaps 100-150,000) troops, cut off the Macedonian supply line at the plain of Issus. Alexander led the cavalry in a charge that broke the Persian center, and Darius fled first. The Persian army collapsed, and Darius's mother, wife and two daughters were captured. Darius offered to cede all lands west of the Euphrates and pay 10,000 talents' ransom, but Alexander refused.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Alexander captures Tyre and conquers Egypt
v.s: Alexander besieged the island city of Tyre for seven months. He built a one-kilometer-long mole from the ruins of old Tyre to reach the island, and constructed fifty-meter-high siege towers and giant battering rams. Of Tyre's 8,000 defenders, most were killed; 30,000 were sold into slavery. He then entered Egypt peacefully, where the priests hailed him as son of Amun. He founded Alexandria at the mouth of the Nile, which became the cultural center of the Hellenistic world.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Battle of Gaugamela: Alexander destroys the Persian Empire
v.s: Darius III assembled a host claimed to be a million (actual perhaps 50-100,000) on the plain of Gaugamela in Assyria, equipped with 200 scythed chariots and fifteen war elephants. Alexander, with 47,000 men, used an oblique tactic to avoid the Persian defensive works, then led the Companion cavalry into the gap in the Persian center. Darius fled again, and the Persian army was utterly defeated. Alexander then entered Babylon, Susa and Persepolis, burning the palace of Xerxes I to avenge the Persian Wars.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Darius III killed; Alexander becomes King of Asia
v.s: Darius III fled to Bactria, where he was imprisoned and murdered by Bessus, satrap of Bactria. Alexander pursued Bessus and executed him for regicide. He gave Darius a royal funeral at Persepolis and proclaimed himself 'King of Asia', adopting Persian court ritual.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Alexander campaigns in Sogdiana, captures Samarkand
v.s: Alexander crossed the Oxus (Amu Darya) into Sogdiana (modern Uzbekistan). He took Samarkand and pursued Bessus to the Jaxartes (Syr Darya), executing him. The Sogdian noble Spitamenes launched a guerrilla war, constantly raiding Macedonian supply lines, and Alexander met with stubborn resistance for the first time.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Alexander kills his close friend Cleitus in a drunken rage
v.s: At a banquet in Samarkand, the veteran Cleitus, drunk, criticized Alexander for adopting Eastern customs and belittling his Macedonian veterans. Alexander seized a guard's spear and killed Cleitus. After sobering up, he wept and beat his chest for three days. The army forgave him, but the incident accelerated his orientalizing policies, including the demand that Persians and Greeks prostrate themselves before him.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Alexander marries the Sogdian princess Roxana and advances into India
v.s: Alexander captured the Sogdian Rock fortress and took Roxana, daughter of the local noble Oxyartes, as his wife, thereby pacifying the Sogdian aristocracy. This was his first Asian wife. He then led his army through the Khyber Pass into the Indus Valley.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Battle of the Hydaspes and the army mutiny
v.s: Alexander defeated the Indian king Porus at the Hydaspes River. Porus, though defeated, was so impressive that Alexander allowed him to continue ruling. When the soldiers learned that ahead lay the Ganges valley and the Nanda kingdom with 6,000 war elephants, they refused to go further. Alexander reluctantly agreed to turn back, marching south along the Indus.

v.t: Second Samnite War breaks out
v.s: Rome and the Samnite alliance resumed war over the status of the Campanian city-states. The Roman consuls marched south, but the Samnites held the high ground of the Apennines, and Rome was at a disadvantage in the early stages.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Macedonian army crosses the Gedrosian Desert
v.s: Alexander led his main army from the mouth of the Indus westward across the Gedrosian Desert (today's Pakistan-Iran border). The region was scorching hot and waterless; the army suffered huge losses from thirst and hunger, with nearly three-quarters of the soldiers and almost all the women and children dying. The admiral Nearchus led the fleet along the coast of the Indian Ocean, drawing the first map of the Arabian Sea.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Alexander returns to Susa and holds mass weddings
v.s: Alexander returned to Susa and organized a 'mass marriage': he himself married Statira, daughter of Darius III, and eighty senior officers married Persian noblewomen. He also announced the full repayment of the army's debts, the discharge of veterans, and the payment of high bonuses. About 10,000 veterans took their discharge and returned to Macedon.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Alexander the Great dies in Babylon
v.s: Alexander, after a bout of heavy drinking in the palace of Nebuchadnezzar in Babylon, developed a high fever and died ten days later at the age of thirty-three. He left no designated heir, saying only that the empire should go 'to the strongest'. The empire immediately fell into the Wars of the Diadochi, splitting into the Hellenistic kingdoms of Seleucid, Ptolemaic, Antigonid, and Lysimachan.

v.t: Lamian War breaks out
v.s: Athens, allied with Aetolia and other Greek states, took advantage of Alexander's death to rebel, besieging the Macedonian general Antipater at Lamia. The Athenian naval commander Leosthenes, with 20,000 men and 200 ships, cut Antipater's supply lines, and the Greek League achieved initial success.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Lamian War ends; Athens surrenders; Aristotle dies
v.s: Macedonian reinforcements returned from Asia and defeated the Greek army at the Battle of Crannon. Athens surrendered unconditionally, handed over Demosthenes and other anti-Macedonian leaders, and accepted a Macedonian garrison and restrictions on democracy. Demosthenes fled to Calauria and took poison. That same year, Aristotle, charged with impiety, fled to Chalcis and died of stomach illness a few months later at age sixty-two. His writings and the Lyceum were inherited by Theophrastus.

v.t: Maurya dynasty founded; Chandragupta unifies northern India
v.s: Chandragupta Maurya, with the help of Chanakya, overthrew the Nanda dynasty and established the Maurya dynasty, with its capital at Pataliputra (modern Patna). He unified most of northern India, creating the first centralized empire in Indian history. His grandson Ashoka would later bring the empire to its peak and convert to Buddhism. The establishment of the Maurya dynasty marked India's transition from the age of kingdoms to the age of empire.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Diadochi wars: Perdiccas's Egyptian campaign fails
v.s: The imperial regent Perdiccas led an army against the Egyptian satrap Ptolemy, but when his troops refused to cross the Nile, he was assassinated by his own officers. Ptolemy I obtained control of Egypt, and Seleucus fled back to Babylon and recovered it. The break-up of the empire became irreversible.

v.t: Rome suffers the disastrous Battle of the Caudine Forks
v.s: During the Second Samnite War, the Roman army was trapped in a narrow defile at the Caudine Forks and surrounded by Samnites. The entire Roman force was forced to surrender, pass under the yoke (a symbolic humiliation), surrender its garrisons, and sign a five-year truce. This was the most shameful defeat in early Roman history, and Rome took five years to recover.

v.t: Maurya Empire established
v.s: Chandragupta Maurya led an army to overthrow the Nanda dynasty, establishing the Maurya Empire. He exploited the political vacuum left by Alexander's withdrawal to occupy the Indus valley, and soon defeated Seleucus I, unifying northern India. The capital Pataliputra became one of the largest cities in the world.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Partition of Triparadisus re-divides the empire
v.s: The victorious Diadochi met at Triparadisus in Syria to redistribute the satrapies: Antipater became European commander, Antigonus became Asian commander and was charged with pursuing Eumenes, Seleucus received Babylonia, Ptolemy retained Egypt. The empire maintained nominal unity for the time being.

d.u: King Xian of Zhou, Ji Bian

v.t: Antipater dies; Polyperchon succeeds
v.s: On his deathbed, Antipater transferred the regency to the elderly general Polyperchon rather than to his own son Cassander. Cassander, enraged, fled to Antigonus for support. The Diadochi wars flared up again, with Cassander forming an alliance with Antigonus and Ptolemy against Polyperchon.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Polyperchon issues the 'Greek Freedom Proclamation'
v.s: To win support from the Greek city-states, Polyperchon declared the abolition of the oligarchies installed by Cassander and the restoration of democracy and freedom to all cities. Athens responded quickly, executing the pro-Macedonian Phocion. But Cassander immediately led a large army against Athens, with naval support from Antigonus.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Cassander controls Athens and executes Phocion
v.s: Cassander's army defeated Polyperchon and occupied Athens. The pro-Macedonian faction regained power, condemned the democratic leader Phocion to death for treason, and he drank poison. Athens later erected a bronze statue to him in repentance. Athens lost its independence again.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Antigonus defeats Eumenes and controls Asia
v.s: Antigonus defeated Eumenes at the Battle of Gabiene, winning by bribing the enemy's Silver Shields. Eumenes was executed, and Antigonus became the effective ruler of Asia Minor, Syria, Babylonia and Media, his power far surpassing that of the other Diadochi.

v.t: Qin conquers Ba and Shu
v.s: King Huiwen of Qin sent Sima Cuo and Zhang Yi to invade Shu via the Stone Ox Road. They defeated the Shu army at Jiameng, annexed Shu and reduced its king to a marquis. Then they proceeded down the Yangtze, extinguished Ba, and established Ba Commandery. Qin gained the grain basket of the Chengdu Plain, doubling its population and wealth, and acquired a strategic rear base for eastward expansion through Hangu Pass.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Seleucus recovers Babylon; all-out war among Antigonus and coalition
v.s: Seleucus raised a revolt in Babylonia, defeated Antigonus' garrison, and recaptured Babylon. Antigonus led his army eastward, and a large-scale war broke out among Antigonus, Seleucus, Ptolemy, Cassander and Lysimachus, plunging the entire Hellenistic world into conflict.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Antigonus proclaims Greek freedom to gain support
v.s: Antigonus, together with Ptolemy and others, signed the 'League of Delos' treaty, declaring all Greek city-states free, in exchange for their neutrality. Cassander was forced to recognize Greek freedom, though he kept garrisons secretly at Corinth and Chalcis.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Cassander besieges Olympius
v.s: To consolidate his control of Greece, Cassander besieged Olympius, a city of the Aetolian League. The Aetolians, using the mountainous terrain, resisted stubbornly. Cassander failed to take the city after a long siege and burned the surrounding fields.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Battle of Gaza and Seleucus recovers Babylon
v.s: Ptolemy and Seleucus defeated Demetrius (son of Antigonus) at Gaza. Seleucus, with only 1,000 men, marched east and within two months recovered Babylon. This battle established Seleucid control over Mesopotamia, and the Seleucid Empire began its rise.

v.t: Construction of the Appian Way begins in Rome
v.s: The censor Appius Claudius Caecus began construction of the Via Appia from Rome to Capua, 200 km in length. The road used a multi-layered foundation: broken stone at the bottom, then a layer of crushed stone mortar, then smoothed stone slabs on top, with drainage ditches on both sides. This was Rome's first military highway, laying the foundation for the Roman road network.

v.t: Rome builds the Appian Way
v.s: The Roman censor Appius Claudius Caecus oversaw the construction of the Via Appia (Appian Way) connecting Rome and Capua, about 212 km long. It was Rome's first road built to 'strategic highway' standards, paved with flat basalt, sturdy and straight, allowing the passage of armies and trade. The Appian Way inaugurated Rome's great road-building tradition, from which the saying 'All roads lead to Rome' derives.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Peace of Babylon temporarily ends Diadochi wars
v.s: Antigonus, Ptolemy, Seleucus, Cassander and Lysimachus signed a peace treaty, each retaining his existing territory. Seleucus received Babylonia, Elam and Media. The peace lasted only one year before Antigonus launched new attacks.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Cassander murders Alexander's surviving family; Agathocles wars against Carthage
v.s: Cassander secretly executed Alexander's widow Roxana and his son Alexander IV, ending the Argead dynasty in Macedon. Meanwhile, the Syracusan tyrant Agathocles led an expedition to Africa, defeating the Carthaginians near Tunis but failing to take Carthage itself.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Cassander founds Thessaloniki
v.s: Cassander founded a new city on the site of ancient Therma, naming it after his wife Thessalonike (Alexander's half-sister). The city was laid out on the Hippodamian grid plan with a square, theater and harbor, becoming an important Macedonian port and commercial center.

d.u: King Shenjing of Zhou, Ji Ding

v.t: Ptolemy conquers Cyprus
v.s: Ptolemy I led a fleet of 200 ships to occupy Cyprus, defeating the Antigonid garrison. Cyprus became an important naval base and shipbuilding center for Ptolemaic Egypt, controlling the eastern Mediterranean trade routes.

d.u: King Nan of Zhou, Ji Yan

v.t: Epicurus founds the 'Garden' school in Athens
v.s: Epicurus bought a garden in Athens and founded a school, allowing women and slaves to enroll. He taught the value of friendship, pleasure and mental tranquility, and opposed superstition and the fear of death. The Garden school stood alongside the Stoic school for two hundred years as one of the major philosophical movements of the Hellenistic age.

v.t: King Wuling of Zhao adopts 'Hu clothing and mounted archery' reform
v.s: To counter the northern nomads and the state of Zhongshan, King Wuling of Zhao, against much opposition, mandated the wearing of Hu-style (narrow-sleeved) clothing and training in mounted archery (cavalry). Zhao established the first independent cavalry force in Chinese history, greatly increasing its military power, and eventually conquered Zhongshan and the Loufan.

d.u: King Nan of Zhou, Ji Yan

v.t: Ptolemy I proclaims himself king in Egypt
v.s: Ptolemy declared himself king at Alexandria, assuming the title 'Savior'. He founded the Ptolemaic dynasty, which ruled Egypt until 30 BC. He created the Library of Alexandria and the Musaeum, attracting scholars such as Euclid, making Alexandria the intellectual center of the Hellenistic world.

d.u: King Nan of Zhou, Ji Yan

v.t: Seleucus I proclaims himself king, establishing the Seleucid Empire
v.s: Seleucus I declared himself king at Babylon, then moved his capital to Seleucia on the Tigris. His empire stretched from the Aegean to the Indus, the largest state in the Hellenistic world. He pursued a policy of Hellenization, founding many Greek-style cities.

d.u: King Nan of Zhou, Ji Yan

v.t: Rome ends the Second Samnite War and regains the advantage
v.s: After ten years of war, Rome finally reversed its defeat at the Caudine Forks, defeated the Samnites at Bovianum, and forced them to sue for peace. Rome gained Campania and northern Samnium, and established colonies along the Via Flaminia.

d.u: King Nan of Zhou, Ji Yan

v.t: Seleucus I allies with the Mauryan emperor Chandragupta
v.s: Seleucus I made a treaty with the Mauryan emperor Chandragupta, ceding the Indus Valley (modern Afghanistan and Pakistan) to Chandragupta in exchange for 500 war elephants. These elephants played a decisive role in the later Battle of Ipsus.

d.u: King Nan of Zhou, Ji Yan

v.t: Cassander and Lysimachus combine forces against Antigonus
v.s: Fearing Antigonus's power, Cassander allied with the Thracian king Lysimachus and attacked Antigonus from two directions in Asia Minor. Ptolemy and Seleucus also joined the anti-Antigonus coalition. The war entered a decisive phase.

d.u: King Nan of Zhou, Ji Yan

v.t: Battle of Ipsus: Antigonus dies; Hellenistic pattern set
v.s: Antigonus and his son Demetrius, with 80,000 infantry, 10,000 cavalry and 75 war elephants, met the allied forces of Seleucus, Lysimachus and Cassander (90,000 men) at Ipsus in Phrygia. Seleucus used his 500 Indian elephants to break Antigonus's cavalry charge. The eighty-year-old Antigonus was hit by a javelin and fell. His empire was partitioned: Seleucus received Syria and eastern Asia Minor; Lysimachus received western Asia Minor; Cassander received Macedon; Ptolemy received Egypt, Palestine and Cyprus. The three-way structure of the Hellenistic world was established.
